---
# ======================================================================
# JACR Manuscript Template
# Journal of the Association for Consumer Research
# ======================================================================
# TWO RENDERING MODES:
#   jacr-mode: manuscript  -- Double-spaced, page-numbered submission format
#   jacr-mode: article     -- Publication-style single-column layout (11pt,
#                             compact spacing, centered page numbers)
#
# To switch modes, change jacr-mode below (or override on command line).
#
# JACR key requirements (see README): total length <= 8,000 words INCLUDING
# references (excluding figure captions, tables, and online appendices);
# abstract under 150 words; avoid shading in tables; use color figures that
# retain contrast in grayscale; online appendix <= 10 MB.
# ======================================================================

title: "Categorical Averaging in Consumer Risk Perception"

# --- Mode selection ---
jacr-mode: manuscript
# jacr-mode: article

# --- Author info (rendered in article mode; omit for anonymous review) ---
author:
  - name:
      literal: "Mehdi Mourali"
  - name:
      literal: "Zhiyong Yang"

# --- Author note (article mode footnote; affiliations and acknowledgments) ---
jacr-author-note: |
  Mehdi Mourali (mehdi.mourali@haskayne.ucalgary.ca) is an associate
  professor of marketing at the Haskayne School of Business, University
  of Calgary. Zhiyong Yang is a professor of marketing at the Farmer
  School of Business, Miami University. Please address correspondence to
  Mehdi Mourali.

# --- Abstract (under 150 words) ---
abstract: |
  How do consumers combine multiple risk items when forming overall risk
  judgments? Drawing on fuzzy trace theory and categorical averaging, this
  research finds that adding a low-risk item to a high-risk item reduces
  overall risk perception because people reason categorically about risk.
  When combining categorical information, consumers tend to average across
  categories instead of adding. Studies in medical and health decision
  contexts show that items in different risk tiers are evaluated
  subtractively, raising the likelihood of choosing the objectively riskier
  option. The effect is eliminated when one probability is exceedingly high,
  when the task requires verbatim probability processing, or when risk is
  presented graphically in a way that emphasizes additivity.

# --- Keywords (optional; JACR guidelines do not specify a count) ---
keywords:
  - risk perception
  - risk communication
  - categorical thinking
  - fuzzy trace theory
  - medical decision-making

format:
  jacrquarto-docx: default
  jacrquarto-pdf: default

bibliography: bibliography.bib

# Optional: override fonts if Times New Roman is available on your system
# mainfont: "Times New Roman"
---

Consumers are increasingly expected to play an active role in their health
and medical care decisions [@hibbard2004], including collaborating with
physicians to define and implement care plans. For consumers to make
informed decisions, they must clearly understand the risks and benefits
associated with different actions.

Health and medical risk information is commonly encountered in the form of
numbers, though verbal and graphical formats are also used [@lipkus2007].
Understanding how consumers perceive and process numerical risk information
has important implications for designing effective risk communications and
facilitating informed decision-making.

# Theoretical Background

A vast literature going back several decades has investigated risk
perception, including its antecedents, moderators, and consequences. In the
medical decision-making domain, most of this work has focused on the
perception of single risks. Less is known about how consumers combine
multiple risk items when forming overall risk impressions
[@leonhardt2018].

## Fuzzy Trace Theory

Fuzzy trace theory [@reyna1995] distinguishes between verbatim and gist
mental representations of information. Verbatim representation is precise
and captures the exact surface form of the information. Gist
representations are less precise and encapsulate the bottom-line meaning.

The theory posits that people prefer to rely on the least precise
representation that still allows them to complete their task. When assessing
an option's riskiness, a basic gist is the categorical distinction between
"low risk" and "high risk."

## Forming Overall Risk Judgments

Information integration theory [@anderson1981] posits that people form
overall impressions of stimuli by engaging in "cognitive algebra," using
simple computational rules to integrate information from multiple cues. We
propose that when combining items with categorically disparate risk levels,
consumers tend to use an averaging rule.

# Study 1: Single Versus Combined Risks

## Method

*Participants and Design.* Three hundred and thirty US residents recruited
from Prolific took part. After excluding participants who failed the
attention check, 295 remained ($M_{\text{age}}$ = 33.5, SD = 12.8).
Participants were randomly assigned to one of three conditions (single vs.
high-low vs. high-high).

## Results and Discussion

A one-way ANCOVA on perceived risk found a significant effect of condition
($F$(2, 290) = 17.53, $p < .001$, $\eta_p^2$ = .11).

|                 | *M*   | SD    | *t*(290) | *p*   |
|:----------------|------:|------:|---------:|------:|
| Single (high)   | 35.3  | 12.4  |          |       |
| Combined (H+L)  | 27.0  | 18.8  | -3.52    | <.001 |
| Combined (H+H)  | 40.6  | 16.1  |  2.29    |  .05  |

: TABLE 1: PERCEIVED RISK ACROSS CONDITIONS (STUDY 1)

::: {.table-notes}
*NOTE.*---Planned contrasts with Bonferroni correction compare each combined
condition to the single condition.
:::

Consumers who read about one high-risk and one low-risk side effect reported
lower overall risk than those who read about a single high-risk side effect.
Those who read about two high-risk side effects perceived greater overall
risk.

# General Discussion

These studies provide converging evidence of consumers' miscalibrated
perceptions of multiple risks, identifying categorical averaging as a key
mechanism through which consumers form perceptions of multiple risks.

## Practical Implications

Presenting multiple risks in non-additive formats leads to miscalibrated
perceptions; changing the presentation mode to formats that visualize the
additive property of multiple risks helps correct the misperception.

<!--
JACR does not print appendices. Any appendix material should live in a
separate online appendix file (see web-appendix.qmd) rather than in the main
document. Information that is essential to the paper should appear alongside
the main text as a table instead. The .appendix class (page break before an
H1) remains available for use inside the online appendix document.
-->
